title: Tabs
summary: Tabs organize related content into separate panels, displaying one section at a time.
preamble: Tabs organize related content into separate panels within the same page or view. Users can switch between sections while maintaining the context of the current page. Each tab displays its associated content panel while the remaining panels stay hidden.
anatomy-overview: Tab consists of a tab bar with individual tab items. Each tab item contains a label and optionally a leading icon. The active tab is visually distinguished from inactive tabs.
anatomy-details:
1. Tab bar
2. Tab item
3. Tab label
4. Active indicator
5. Leading icon (optional)

columns.content:
Use tabs when content can be divided into distinct sections that belong to the same context and hierarchy level.

Examples:

- Account details, Transactions, and Documents
- Overview, Holdings, and Activity
- Personal information, Contact details, and Preferences

Tabs work best when the number of options is limited. As the number of tabs increases, they become harder to scan and compare. If many options are required, consider whether another pattern would better support the user's task. When tabs exceed the available width, the tab bar becomes horizontally scrollable.

Tab items can include optional leading and trailing content. Use leading content, such as icons or avatars, to help identify a tab. Use trailing content, such as badges or counts, to provide additional context about the tab's content.

overview.title: Tabs vs. segmented control
overview.section-content:
Tabs and segmented controls may appear similar but serve different purposes.

Use tabs when switching between different content panels.

Examples:

- Overview, Transactions, and Documents
- Personal details, Contact details, and Security settings

Use segmented control when changing a setting, mode, filter, or representation of content.

Examples:

- List view or Grid view
- Week, Month, or Year
- Amount or Percentage

Think of tabs as organizing content into sections, while segmented controls change how content is displayed.

dolist.text: Use tabs to separate related content panels, such as Overview, Transactions, and Documents.
Use tabs when users need to switch between sections while staying in the same context.
Use trail slot with for example badges to indicate additional information, such as unread items or counts.
Use leading content, such as icons or avatars, when it helps users identify a section.

dontlist.text: Don't use tabs for List view and Grid view, use a segmented control instead.
Don't use tabs to navigate between pages with different URLs.
Don't use tabs to filter, sort, or change the presentation of the same content.
Don't use icons, avatars, or badges purely for decoration.

ux-text.title: Writing UX text for tabs
ux-text.section-content:
Tab labels serve as both navigation menu items and implicit sub-headings for the content they reveal. This means labels must be clear and descriptive enough to stand on their own — without requiring a sub-heading beneath them.

Do not capitalise the first letter of every word in tab labels. Use uppercase only for:

- The first letter of the first word
- Proper names
- Abbreviations

ux-text.title: Tab labels
ux-text.section-content: Because tab labels also act as sub-headings, they must be explicit and self-explanatory. Avoid vague or generic labels that do not describe the content.

ux-text.title: Common tab labels in overviews
ux-text.section-content:
Use consistent and recognisable labels for common content groupings. Standard tab labels used in overviews include:

- **Value** – presents details about the current holding
- **Payments** – shows upcoming, pending, and historical payments
- **About the account** – shows details about the account or service, such as connected services, interest rates, and fees

When applicable, include the subject area in the label, for example *About the loan* or *Fund transactions*.
